reading.opening: Every great system begins with someone pressing Enter without knowing what comes next.
reading.today: Today is a hello world day.
reading.body: Start the thing you've been afraid to start. The first version will be embarrassing. That's the entire point.

dont_list: refactor before you've written it once
ask three friends what they think

reading.opening: You have all the tools you need. The shame would be in pretending otherwise.
reading.body: The thing you've been meaning to make — make it. Badly. Now. The polish comes later, or it doesn't come, and that's also fine.

do_list: open the terminal you've been avoiding
write the prototype in one sitting
dont_list: ask permission for a side project

reading.opening: The answer was already written. Someone, somewhere, three years ago, in a comment you've never read.
reading.body: Stop guessing. Open the docs. The thing you're stuck on is solved on page four of something boring.

do_list: read the README before you ask
dont_list: copy from Stack Overflow without reading

reading.opening: The system that holds everything together is older than you, and it does not need your gratitude.
reading.body: Resources are available. The infrastructure works. Use what is already there before you build something new.

dont_list: "what if we used a different framework"
optimize what is already fast enough

reading.opening: Structure is not creativity's enemy. Structure is what makes creativity not a mess.
reading.body: Write the schema before the feature. Draw the diagram before the meeting. The boring document you avoid is the one that will save you in six months.

reading.opening: The way it's always been done is not always wrong. Sometimes the tradition is the wisdom.
reading.body: Ask the senior engineer the question you're embarrassed to ask. Read the style guide. The boring conventions exist because someone, once, suffered.

reading.opening: Two engineers, one keyboard. Either it becomes love or it becomes a postmortem.
reading.body: The bug you can't see, someone else will see in five minutes. The pride of debugging alone is not worth the four hours.

do_list: ask for a second pair of eyes

lucky.companion: XX the code review after review

reading.opening: Force of will is a renewable resource, but only just barely.
reading.body: The thing has to ship. Not perfect, not elegant — shipped. You can apologize for the technical debt later. You cannot apologize for the missed deadline.

reading.opening: The real strength is not crushing the junior engineer's PR. It is asking, gently, why they made the choice they made.
reading.body: Be the reviewer you wish you had at 22. The criticism that lands is the criticism that arrives with a question, not a verdict.

do_list: explain the why, not the what

reading.opening: You are tending a forest no one remembers. The day it burns is the day they will learn your name — and misspell it.
reading.body: Don't ask for credit. Don't explain what you do. The system runs because you are watching. That is enough, and also not enough.

do_list: merge the dependency bump nobody noticed
write the doc you've been postponing
dont_list: explain your job to your parents again

reading.opening: Every day is mostly the same day. Then one of them, without warning, decides your year.
reading.body: The same standup, the same status, the same lunch. Show up anyway. The unremarkable days are doing more work than you think.

do_list: give your status in under 60 seconds

reading.opening: The metrics will be reviewed. The metrics have always been reviewed. The only question is whether you reviewed them first.
reading.body: Look at the numbers honestly. Not the ones you want to see — the other ones. The truth is usually less dramatic than the silence around it.

do_list: own your mistake before they find it

lucky.companion: XX the code review after review

reading.opening: You are waiting for them to move. They are waiting for you to move. The standoff itself is the choice.
reading.body: Don't push. Don't message. Don't escalate. The thing you think requires urgency mostly requires patience.

reading.opening: The function you wrote three years ago has served you. Today, you delete it. This is not loss. This is making room.
reading.body: Remove the dead code. Archive the old project. Unsubscribe from the newsletter you no longer read. Endings are how systems stay alive.

do_list: write the eulogy in the commit message

reading.opening: Slowly. The system is being rebuilt while it runs. There is no version with a celebration at the end. The celebration is that it kept running.
reading.body: Make one small improvement. Then another. The ten-year codebase was not refactored in a weekend, and yours won't be either.

do_list: write the test before the fix
leave it cleaner than you found it

reading.opening: You are paying for decisions made by people who no longer work here. This is the job.
reading.body: The bug is in code that pre-dates you. The architecture is from before the company knew what it was. Fix what you can. Forgive the rest.

do_list: read the git blame with compassion
write a small fix, not a big one
accept what cannot be changed today

reading.opening: The thing that breaks today was already broken. You're just the one who got the page.
reading.body: Don't try to fix it before you understand it. Don't blame the last engineer. They were as scared as you are now. Roll back. Sleep. Read the postmortem in the morning.

dont_list: push a hotfix you wrote in panic

lucky.companion: XX the code review after review

reading.opening: Your day job pays you. Your night project defines you. Today, give it the small hour it needs.
reading.body: The thing you build for no one is the thing that will eventually find its someone. Don't quit. Don't ship. Just open the file.

do_list: spend 30 minutes on the side thing
write down the idea you had in the shower
show it to one trusted friend
dont_list: monetize it before you love it
post about it on twitter yet

reading.opening: The pager will go off. The question is only when, and whether you'll be sober.
reading.body: Stay reachable. Stay close to your laptop. The night is not your friend, but it is your responsibility. Sleep with one eye on the screen.

dont_list: go more than 20 minutes from wifi
volunteer to take someone else's shift

reading.opening: The release is out. The metrics are green. For one afternoon, briefly, you are a god.
reading.body: Take the win. Tell your mother. Post the screenshot. Tomorrow there will be a bug, but tomorrow is tomorrow.

reading.opening: Someone is reading what you wrote with fresh eyes. They will see what you no longer can.
reading.body: The comments are not attacks. They are gifts written badly. Receive them. Reply with grace. The pull request that taught you something is the one that mattered.

do_list: ask "can you say more" instead of defending
accept the suggestion you don't like

reading.opening: The smallest version that works is the most beautiful version that exists.
reading.body: The thing is done. It is small, and it is real, and it is enough. Don't add the feature. Don't polish the UI. Show it to a user. The next version starts tomorrow.

dont_list: compare to v2 in your head
